coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) through the fluidized bed thermal treatment of residual biomass assisted by γ-alumina
coredata.dc:creator: Brachi, Paola
coredata.dc:description: An innovative route for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is presented herein, which relies on the use of the fluidized bed technology and commercial γ-alumina as a catalyst, for the first time. Specifically, sugar beet pulp, which is the solid by-product of sugar beet industry, has been successfully tested for the synthesis of self-functionalized carbon nanoparticles under mild conditions of temperature (300 °C) and time (30 min). Different techniques including TEM, UV–vis, fluorescence, Raman and XPS spectroscopy have been applied to characterize the as-prepared CQDs. The formation of CQDs on γ-alumina is discussed and a possible mechanism is proposed. A solid biofuel is also obtained as a co-product of the process. The innovative finding that a catalyst can be used to produce CQDs from the volatiles driven off the biomass by heating opens up new possibilities of valorisation for the off-gas substream coming from other biomass conversion processes (e.g., torrefaction).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-based carbon dots (CQDs)
γ-Alumina catalyst
Agro-industrial residues
Dry thermal synthesis
Fluidized bed technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fresh and coked γ-alumina spheres characterization
Structure, chemical composition and optical properties of SBP-based CQDs
Properties as a fuel of raw and thermally treated sugar beet pulp
Mechanism of formation of CQDs on γ-Al2O3 catalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictures of: a. the experimental apparatus used for the synthesis of biomass-based CQDs assisted by γ-alumina (Al2O3 spheres with a Sauter mean diameter of about 680 μm); b. untreated binary mixture of sugar beet pulp and alumina spheres γ-alumina; c. colored alumina bed arising from fluidized bed synthesis performed 300 °C; d. colored alumina bed arising from synthesis performed 300 °C in a fixed bed reactor; e. a higher magnification of the inset shown in Fig. 1b; f. a higher magnification of the inset shown in Fig. 1c and g. a higher magnification of the inset shown in Fig. 1d.

Pictures of SBP-based CQDs obtained from both fixed and fluidized bed synthesis when dissolved in different solvents (i.e., water and ethanol) illuminated under regular (a, c; daylight lamp) and UV light (b, d; λ = 395 nm). Solution have been prepare by contacting for few seconds about 1 g of colored γ-Al2O3 spheres with 5 mL of solvent.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the ethanolic solution of the CQDs, which have been obtained through thermal treatment of sugar beet pulp at 300 °C in (a) the fluidized bed (FB) and (b) the fixed bed (FX) reactor configuration.
(a) XPS, (b) C1 s, (c) O1 s, and (d) N1 sspectra of the as-produced FB-CQDs.
Derivative C KLL spectrum for FX (a) and the FB (b) SBP-based CQDs.
Raman spectra for FX and FB systems.

Comparison between the UV–vis spectra of the dilute (10-fold dilution) aqueous solutions of FB-CQDs and FX-CQDS. In the inset, a magnification of the UV–vis spectra of the as-prepared acqueous solution (not diluted solution of CQDs in water shown in Fig. 2a) over the range 350–600 nm is also provided.
Steady-state Emission spectra for FX- (a) and FB- (b) CQDs, collected at different excitation wavelengths.
Synchronous mode Emission spectra of FX-CQDs (a) and FB-CQDs (b) in water, as collected at different excitation wavelengths.
Comparison between excitation spectra for FB and FX CQDs dispersed in water.
Decay curve of the as-prepared FX-CQDs (a) and FB-CQDs (b) in water, collected at different emission wavelengths.
Ultimate composition of colored γ-Al2O3 beds and monochromatic spheres (i.e., white, yellow, brown and dark brown spheres) manually selected from the same alumina beds.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The ultimate composition of fresh γ-alumina spheres is provided as reference.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Values of the D parameter for FX-CQDs and FB-CQDs.
Decay times and relative population for deconvolution of FX-CQD.
Decay times and relative population for deconvolution of FB-CQDs.
Main properties of raw and thermally treated SBP as a fuel.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) through the fluidized bed thermal treatment of residual biomass assisted by γ-alumina

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Research on Combustion, National Research Council, P.le Tecchio 80, 80125, Napoli, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Research on Combustion, National Research Council, P.le Tecchio 80, 80125 Napoli, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative route for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is presented herein, which relies on the use of the fluidized bed technology and commercial γ-alumina as a catalyst, for the first time. Specifically, sugar beet pulp, which is the solid by-product of sugar beet industry, has been successfully tested for the synthesis of self-functionalized carbon nanoparticles under mild conditions of temperature (300 °C) and time (30 min). Different techniques including TEM, UV–vis, fluorescence, Raman and XPS spectroscopy have been applied to characterize the as-prepared CQDs. The formation of CQDs on γ-alumina is discussed and a possible mechanism is proposed. A solid biofuel is also obtained as a co-product of the process. The innovative finding that a catalyst can be used to produce CQDs from the volatiles driven off the biomass by heating opens up new possibilities of valorisation for the off-gas substream coming from other biomass conversion processes (e.g., torrefaction).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) – also called fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) or carbon dots (CDs) – are a new class of carbon nanomaterials that exhibit physical and optical properties analogous to those of classical heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1]. Compared to the latter ones, however, carbon-based quantum dots have the distinctive features of prominent biocompatibility, low production cost, low toxicity, great environmental sustainability, easy functionalization, excellent water solubility, colorful photoluminescence and the electrical properties of sp2 carbon material, which make them a very interesting alternative to QDs [2]. CQDs were accidently discovered by Xu et al. [3] in 2004 during the purification of single walled carbon nanotubes from arc-discharged soot. Since then, a wide variety of synthesis routes have been developed for the preparation of CQDs and much progress has been achieved regarding their functionalization and technical applications [3–5]. The vast majority of CQDs uses are in the field of medical diagnostics and bioimaging [6,7]. However, recently, CQDs have also been successfully employed in photocatalysis [8–11], the development of novel materials for optoelectronic devices (e.g., solar cells [12] and LEDs [13]) and energy storage applications [14].

The main synthesis routes currently adopted for the production of carbon quantum dots can be classified in two main categories, namely [1–3]: i). the top-down approach that involves “breaking” macroscopic carbon structures, such as graphite, activated carbon and carbon nanotubes through treatments like arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation (generally involving the use of strongly oxidizing acids), and ultrasonic synthesis; and ii.) the bottom-up approach, which relies on forming CQDs from molecular precursors such as citric acid, sucrose and glucose via microwave-assisted syntheses, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, template-based routes and plasma treatments. Advantages and disadvantages of each synthesis route are discussed in depth elsewhere [1,5]. Compared to top-down approaches, the bottom-up methods have advantages in terms of sustainability and compliance with green-chemistry principles, in particular when biomass precursors are used as a carbon source [12]. In particular, due to its versatility and simple experimental setup, which avoids the need of strongly toxic chemicals and the further passivation of the dots surface, hydrothermal carbonization has now become the most common procedure adopted for the production of CQDs from natural biomass or other readily available carbon sources [15]. Although hydrothermal methods are very versatile in the preparation of various nanomaterials [16–19], they still suffer from multiple disadvantages regarding the commercialization of commodity chemicals [20], including the high cost of the stainless steel autoclaves that are used as reactors, the difficulty of real-time reaction monitoring due to their closed (or “black-box”) reaction processes, and the safety risks that are associated with the high-temperature and high-pressure reaction conditions. Therefore, for the industrial scale-up, it is important to develop more easy and scalable method for synthesizing high-quality CDs.
Herein, an innovative method for the synthesis of biomass-based CDs is described, which involves the use of the fluidized bed technology and commercial γ-alumina as a catalyst, for the first time.

Carbon deposits are well known to cover the γ-alumina surface when exposed to relatively high temperatures in presence of biomass feedstocks by means of coking and/or fouling mechanisms [21–23]. This phenomenon, which is extremely undesirable when alumina is used as a catalyst or an adsorbent material, has been successfully exploited in the present work for the sustainable synthesis of self-functionalized carbon nanodots by using an agro-industrial residue as a carbon source.

Compared to more traditional wet-chemistry-based approaches (e.g., hydro/solvothermal methods), the synthesis of CQDs catalysed by γ-alumina avoids the use of harsh solvents, eliminates the safety risks associated with high-temperature and high-pressure reaction conditions and offers a single-step synthetic route, which may increase the potential for the sustainable large scale production of biomass-based CQDs.

Again, unlike other bottom-up techniques [1–3], a high value by-product is also obtained during the synthesis of biomass-based CQDs assisted by γ-Al2O3, i.e. the residual thermally treated biomass, which can be valorized as a high quality solid biofuel due to its lower oxygen-to-carbon ratio and, therefore, higher calorific value compared to the parent biomass. In a properly designed and operated synthesis process, such a fuel may be combusted to partially deliver the process heat demand, thus increasing the overall efficiency.

The innovative finding of the present research, namely that a catalyst can be used to produce CQDs from the volatile matter that is driven off the biomass by heating, opens up new possibilities of valorisation for the off-gas substream (pyrolytic volatiles, torgas, tar, etc.) coming from other biomass conversion processes, such as torrefaction [24–26], slow pyrolysis [27] and carbonization [28].

In the specific case of the synthesis of CQDs assisted by γ-alumina, where it is necessary to separate the residual biomass from the catalytic bed, to isolate the CDs and regenerate the exhausted Al2O3 particles, the fluidized bed technology allows for a continuous operation and a more efficient use of the catalysts, with obvious advantages [29] for large-scale applications.

Air-dried sugar beet pulp (SBP) residues (less than 10%wt. moisture content) in their original particle size and shape (i.e., flat and elongated particles with a length in the range of 0–2 cm and a width in the range of 0-0.5 cm) have been used as a carbon source in the synthesis of CQDs assisted by γ-alumina (see the Fig. 1 b and e); the residues were collected from a sugar industry located in Bologna (Italy) in November 2018. Commercial γ-alumina spheres (Puralox 0.6/170 by SASOL) with a Sauter mean diameter of about 680 μm has been used as a catalyst; Sasol Germany GmbH kindly provided them. Two solvents of different polarity, which are arranged below in order of their increasing polarity, have been tested to extract the as-prepared self-functionalized CQDs from the γ-alumina surface, namely: ethanol (Ethyl alcohol, Pure 200 proof, anhydrous, ≥ 99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich) < distilled water.

A picture of the bench-scale apparatus used for the one-step fluidized bed synthesis of CQDs assisted by γ-alumina is shown in Fig. 1a. It consists of semi-batch reactor made of a steel tubular column (38 mm inner diameter and 350 mm height) heated by means of an electrical heating tape (FGR-100/240 V V-ROPE HEATER 500 W by Omegalux). The temperature of the reactor is regulated by a proportional integral derivative (PID) controller (Gefran 600 PID), which reads the bed temperature through a K-type thermocouple inserted in the reactor at 7 cm above the gas distributor. A disk-shaped ceramic fiber felt (6 mm thick) set at the bottom of the column and topped by a 15 mm high bed of steel spheres (4.5 mm diameter) acts as the gas distributor. A flowmeter (Asameter Model E, by ASA) with a 100–1000 NL/h flow range supplies cold nitrogen as a fluidization gas during tests. Specifically, the carrier gas percolates the bed upward and leaves the reactor from the top.

In a typical experimental run, the reactor is loaded with approximately 100 mL of air-dried SBP uniformly mixed with 150 mL of γ-alumina spheres (see Fig. 1b). The synthesis of CQDs is performed at 300 °C by ensuring a reaction time of 30 min. Nitrogen is used as a fluidizing gas at a flow rate of 450 N L/h, corresponding to a superficial gas velocity of about 0.212 m/s at 300 °C (about twice the minimum fluidization velocity of the γ-alumina spheres that is equal to 1.104 m/s at 300 °C). The selection of the operating conditions to properly fluidize (without slugging and channeling) the alumina/biomass binary mixture rely on findings from previous investigations [26,30–32], where the fluidization and the segregation behavior of polydisperse binary mixtures has been deeply investigated by using both visual observations and pressure drop measurements.

Once the prefixed test time is passed, the bed is cooled down to room temperature as fast as possible (approximately 5 min) by turning the electrical heater off, removing the reactor from the insulating cylinder, and blowing cold compressed air onto the reactor surface; the binary mixture (bed) is then collected from the reactor. It is worth noting that, in the present study, the test time is defined as the sum of the heating time (i.e., the time during which the bed is heated from the onset decomposition temperature of SBP - as obtained by TGA runs [25], i.e., 135 °C - to the test temperature) and the holding time (i.e., the time during which the biomass is kept at the preset test temperature). In keeping with Bergman et al. [33] the cooling time (i.e., the time during which the temperature decreased to the onset decomposition again) has been considered to have a negligible effect; it is expected that biomass decomposition reactions stop as soon as the temperature is decreased, since the most thermally labile or reactive parts of the biomass feedstock have already reacted.

The alumina spheres, which during the thermal treatment undergo coking and fouling processes [21–23] accompanied by the formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are subsequently separated from the partially converted biomass by manual sieving. The recovery of CQDs from the surface of the “colored” (coked) alumina spheres is achieved by exploiting the unique feature of the functionalized carbon nanoparticles to be soluble in water and/or organic media. No additional surface functionalization steps are required to make the as-prepared CQDs photoluminescent as well as soluble in water and/or other organic solvents. In fact, a highly fluorescent solution (under UV irradiation) containing self-functionalized carbon nanoparticles is obtained by simply contacting the spheres with distilled water or a polar organic solvent (i.e., ethanol) for a short amount of time (typically few seconds).

Spent alumina spheres remaining after the recovery of CQDs by solvent extraction are finally regenerated by burning off residual carbonaceous deposits under controlled conditions [34]. As a co-product of the process, a thermally a treated biomass is also obtained.
TEM observations were performed on the as-prepared CQDs by using a FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit TWIN, a 20–120 kV/LaB6 Transmission Electron Microscope.
UV-Vis spectra were recorded by a Jasco V-660 UV–Vis spectrometer using quartz cuvettes of an optical path length of 10 mm, in the spectral range of 190–900 nm, with a slid width of 2 nm.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Fluorolog 3 (Horiba- JobinYvon) spectrofluorometer, with 2 nm grids for both excitation and emission. The emissions have been collected in the range 270–700 nm, exciting the sample with variable wavelengths from 250 nm to 450 nm. The corresponding excitation spectra have been collected in the range 250–500 nm, under an excitation wavelength of 550 nm, with 1 nm grids. The emissions have also been collected, in the synchronous mode, in the range 270–600 nm, with 1 nm grids for excitation and 2 nm grids for emission, exciting the sample with variable wavelengths from 250 nm to 430 nm.

Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were carried out by a time-correlated single- photon-counting (TCSPC) system (Horiba-Jobin-Yvon). Time-resolved measurements were carried on exciting the samples using a 405 nm pulsed laser diode and collecting the emission decay at different emission wavelengths (460 nm, 490 nm and 550 nm). The fluorescence decay profiles were analyzed through decay analysis software (DAS6a HORIBA Scientific) to a multiexponential decay equation. The quality of the fits was checked by examining the residual distribution and the χ2 value. All experiments were performed at room temperature using quartz cuvettes with an optical path length of 10 mm.

Raman measurements were performed at room temperature, in the range 100-3000 cm−1, with a micro-Raman spectrometer (μ-Raman) Renishaw RM 2000 equipped with a cooled CCD detector in conjunction with a Leica microscope. A 20× objective has been used to focus the laser light on the samples and to collect the Raman signal. The excitation light was the 514.5 nm line of an argon ion laser, chosen in order to minimize the luminescence background. The maximum power has been about 0.6 mW to avoid annealing effect in the sample. The acquisitions were carried out in dynamic mode with 10 accumulations of 1 s for each point analyzed, obtaining a good signal-to-noise ratio. The spectra were calibrated using the 520.5 cm−1 peak of a Si crystal and the spectrometer resolution was about 2 cm−1.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by an Escalab Mk II (VG Scientific, UK) spectrometer, equipped with a standard Al K α excitation source and a 5-channeltron detection system. The uncertainty of the binding energy (BE) was ±0.1 eV. Spectroscopic data have been processed by the CasaXPS software, subtracting the Shirley background and using a peak-fitting routine with Voigt functions.

The determination of the proximate composition (i.e., moisture content (M), volatile matter (VM), fixed carbon (FC), and ashes (ASH) of both fresh and thermally-treated solids (i.e., biomass samples and alumina particles) was performed by using a TGA 701 LECO thermogravimetric analyser.

The elemental composition (CHN) was obtained by means of a CHN 2000 LECO analyser. The oxygen content was estimated by subtracting the sum of the percentages (dry basis) of C, H, N and ash from 100%. The higher heating value (HHV, MJ/kg on a dry basis) of raw and thermally treated biomass samples was measured using an oxygen bomb calorimeter (Parr Model 6200).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fresh and coked γ-alumina spheres characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1c shows the pictures of the “colored” (coked) γ-alumina spheres obtained through the above-described procedure. For comparison, a picture of a sample of γ-alumina spheres, which have been obtained under the same operating conditions but using a fixed bed reactor configuration, is shown in Fig. 1d; specifically, this means that the nitrogen flow rate was set equal to 100 Nl/h in order to ensure a superficial gas velocity (i.e., 0.047 m/s at 300 °C) lower than the minimum fluidization velocity of the alumina spheres. The pics highlight that the fluidized bed technology ensures a more uniform and, hence, efficient use of the catalyst compared to the fixed bed one. An evidence of this is provided by the more uniform deposition of carbonaceous material on the catalyst surface, which, in turn is revealed by the more homogeneous color of the alumina bed arising from the fluidized bed synthesis (see Fig. 1f. vs. g.) as well as the narrower variation in the elemental composition of carbon residues deposited on the alumina spheres of different colors (see Table 1 ) that were manually selected from the bed samples shown in Fig. 1c and d. The greater heterogeneity of the colored γ-alumina bed in Fig. 1g, which is most likely the consequence of flow channeling phenomena typically occurring in packed beds, obviously implies a worse control of the synthesis process compared to the fluidized bed configuration [29,30].

It is worth noting that, despite the abovementioned advantages in using the fluidized bed technology for the implementation of this specific process, major challenge exists in the selection of the proper operating conditions. The fluidized bed reactor can be operated over a wide range of gas flow rates, thereby allowing selection of the optimum contact time or residence time of gas and solid particles [35]. However, for gas-phase catalytic reactions in a gas-solid fluidized-bed, the reactor performances are not only affected by the reaction kinetics, but also by the hydrodynamics, which can markedly influence the gas-solids mass transfer and contacting and, hence, the extent of the reaction [35]. For instance, it is well-known that the operation at a relatively low-velocity fluidization or bubbling fluidization regime results in the formation of a dense phase flow structure with dispersed bubbles, which not only act as agitators enhancing the gas-solid mixing but also cause the gas bypasses the bed without effectively contacting solid particles. Accordingly, further hydrodynamic investigations are needed to optimize the reactor performances. A comprehensive study on the performances of various types of fluidization regimes (particulate, bubbling/slugging, turbulent, fast (circulating) and pneumatic transport fluidization) in relation to gas-phase catalytic reactions can be found elsewhere [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, chemical composition and optical properties of SBP-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the pictures of the aqueous (a. and b.) and the ethanolic dispersions (c. and d.) of the as-prepared CQDs, when illuminated by natural light (Fig. 2a, c; daylight) and UV radiation (Fig. 2b, d; λ = 395 nm). The pics highlight that for both the CQDs samples (i.e., CQDs from the fixed and the fluidized bed experiments, which are coded below as FX-CQDs and FB-CQDs, respectively) and for each of the tested solvents, a bright blue fluorescent solution is obtained under irradiation with 395 nm ultraviolet (UV) light, which suggests that the as-prepared SBP-based CQDs exhibit an excellent solubility both in the water and the organic media having lower polarity.

It is worth noting that no fluorescence was observed by replacing the γ-alumina bed with inert sand as well as in the case of spheres treated at temperature levels lower than 250 °C; this suggests that γ-Al2O3 could not be active in synthesis of self-functionalized carbon quantum dots at temperatures lower than 250 °C.

The size and the morphology of the as-prepared CQDs have been examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations performed on the ethanolic solutions shown in Fig. 2c. In particular, Fig. 3 a–b shows the TEM images and the size distributions (i.e., histogram and Gaussian fit) of the as-prepared CQDs as determined through a statistical analysis performed on more than two thousands particles by using the ImageJ software. The Gaussian fitting curve reveals that the average size of the as-prepared CQDs is 0.9 ± 0.4 nm in the case of the fluidized bed (FB) reactor configuration and 1.1 ± 0.3 nm in the case of the fixed bed (FX) reactor configuration. The formation of ultra-small (mean diameter ≤ 1 nm) carbon quantum dots is common to other synthetic routes (e.g., laser irradiation and electrophoreses) reported in the literature [37]. Specifically, the limited size of the as-prepared CQDs may be due to the small pore size in the coked alumina catalysts; a similar phenomenon has already been well-demonstrated in the case of carbon deposits formed over zeolite catalysts [38]. It is worth noting that as coke accumulates within the catalyst pores, the effective diameter of the pores decreases, leading to an increase of the resistance to the transport of reactants and products in the pores. If coke is concentrated near the pore mouth it will be more effective as a barrier than the same amount evenly distributed on the pore wall, and eventually pore blockage can occur [39].

TEM micrograph in Fig. 3a also reveals that the as-prepared FB-CQDs consist of well-dispersed nanoparticles having a rather uniform spherical shape. Conversely, the TEM micrograph of FX-CQDs reveals the presence of some nanoparticle aggregates (Fig. 3b), which results in a slightly broader particle size distribution (up to 5.0 nm).

The chemical structure and composition of the as-prepared SBP-based CQDs have been characterized by XPS and Raman techniques. In more details, the XPS survey spectrum of FB-CQDs reveals the presence of four predominant peaks at 101.8, 284.6, 400.0, 532.0 eV corresponding to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s elements respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The in-depth analysis of the spectrum demonstrates that FB-CQDs are composed of C, N, O and Si elements with the atomic percentages of 88.9, 0.5, 8.1 and 2.5%, respectively. The remarkably high value of the C/O ratio (≈ 11) and presence of Si in FB-CQDs is ascribed to the presence of impurities in the CQDs powder, which was obtained by freeze drying an aqueous solution not preliminary subjected to any purification steps, such as centrifugation, filtration, etc. The peak-fitting analysis of C 1s signal (Fig. 4b) reveals the presence of CC bond (at 284.6 eV), C–O bond (at 286.7 eV), and carboxylic group (at 288.2 eV), whereas the O 1s signal (Fig. 4c) show two deconvoluted peaks at 532.0 and 534.4 eV, belonging to OC/O-H− and CO bonds. Fig. 4d displays a single peak at 400.0 eV derived from N1s signal, which can be attributed to the C–N bond. The XPS survey spectrum of FX-CQDs (not shown here for the sake of brevity) reveals the presence of the same three predominant peaks at 284.6, 399.2 and 531.3 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s elements respectively. The in-depth analysis of the spectrum demonstrates that FX-CQDs consist of C, N, and O elements, with the C/O and C/N atomic ratios equal to 2.3 and 21.8, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of the FB-CQDs and FX-CQDs samples, which are quite similar. In particular, the Raman bands located at about 1400 and 1650 cm−1 in both the spectra are associated with the typical D-band (at ≈1340–1400 cm−1) and the G-band (at ≈ 1560–1700 cm−1) of carbon structures, respectively [41]. The G band is a characteristic feature of the graphitic layers and corresponds to the tangential vibration of the of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, whereas the D band corresponds to the disordered carbon and defects in the graphitic structure [42] associated with heteroatoms doping defects and reduced size [43]; it represents a ring breathing mode from sp2 carbon rings, although to be active the ring must be adjacent to a graphene edge or a defect [44]. In general, the peak intensities of D-band and G-band reflect the amount of disordered and ordered carbon structure, respectively. In other words, the higher the intensity ratio of D-band to G-band the lower the degree of graphitization and the higher the amount of disordered carbon structures. Based on the Raman spectra shown in Fig. 6, the ID/IG ratios of FX-CQDs and FB-CQDs were calculated to be 1.79 and 1.89, thus suggesting the presence of numerous defects in the core of the carbon quantum dots; such defect-rich graphene-like structure can be ascribed to the small size of the as-prepared CQDs, which confirms the TEM results (see Fig. 3).

The optical properties of FX-CQDs and FB-CQDs in aqueous solution were investigated by Fluorescence analysis and UV analysis. In particular, the UV–vis measurements were performed on the as prepared aqueous solution shown in Fig. 2a and on the same solution, after a 10-fold dilution with distilled water. Similar to other CQDs reported in literature [45,46], the as-prepared CQDs exhibit a strong optical absorption in the UV region (260–320 nm) with a tail extending into the visible region (cf. Fig. 7 ). In more details, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FB-CQDs exhibits a strong absorption below 300 nm with a maximum peak at 260 nm that is ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon core and a broad shoulder at about 315 nm, which is assigned to the n-π* transition of the surface functional groups, containing N and O-bonds; this is consistent with the results of the XPS characterization. The UV–vis spectrum of FX sample shows the same characteristic bands of the FB sample, but with a small blue shift in the band position. In addition, the absorbance of both the as-prepared samples was found to extend to longer wavelength in the range of 350–550 nm at higher concentration of FX-CQDs and FB-CQDs in water; in fact, a broad low-intensity band at 435 and 456 nm just appears in the UV–vis spectra of the undiluted solutions. The broadness of the bands, especially those centered at 315 nm and 430 nm could be due to the presence of overlapped signals.

Steady-state fluorescence measurements were also performed on the diluted solution of the as prepared-CQDs (10-fold dilution respect to the original concentration). Fig. 8 a–b show the emission spectra of the as-prepared CQDs in water, as obtained by exciting the solutions between 320 nm and 420 nm and collecting the emissions in the range 330–600 nm. Steady state fluorescence studies highlighted that FX-CQDs in water presents emission bands in the range 385–500, the shifting depending on the excitation-wavelength. In addition to such excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior, which is common to other CQDs [47,48] and cluster systems [49] reported in literature, the photoluminescence (PL) emission profile of FX-CQDs (Fig. 8a) also revealed the presence of an emission peak centered at about 450 nm, which seems to be independent to the excitation-wavelength. It is worth noting the excitation-independent PL is a common feature in nitrogen-doped carbon dots [50,51].

To better evaluate the shifting behavior of the emission bands, the fluorescence behavior of the as prepared CQDs was also studies in the synchronous mode, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 a–b. The in-depth analysis of the synchronous emission spectrum in Fig. 9a puts in evidence the presence of a complex emission behavior. In particular, by exciting the system between 250 nm and 330 nm, the fluorescence emission grows in intensity and shifts its position from 370 to 390 nm. Then, continuing to excite the system, the position of the emission maximum shifts, decreasing its intensity, until about 430 nm. Furthermore, for excitation upper to 330 nm, a new emission signal appears in the spectrum, centered at about 450 nm, which is independent from the excitation wavelength. This complex emission behavior can be attributed to the engrossment of multiple discrete electronic states due to the presence of different types of aggregates. Studies by Sharma et al. [52] highlighted that the surface exposed functional groups play a crucial role in determining the extent and nature of aggregation and thus the occurrence of excitation dependent or -independent emission spectra. The emission behavior of the FB-CQDs sample has been also analyzed, in steady state (Fig. 8b) and in synchronous mode (Fig. 9b). Results show that the fluorescence behavior of FB and FX systems is very similar; in more details, even for FB system is possible to observe the presence of an exciting wavelength-dependant emission, with maximum position shifting from 380 to 430 nm (exciting between 300 and 380 nm) and a fixed emission, at about 450 nm, clearly visible exciting the system at wavelengths above 330 nm. Also in this case, the presence of different fluorescence behaviors could be related to the engrossment of multiple discrete electronic states associated with the presence of different types of aggregates. It is worth noting that emission peak centered at about 450 nm, which was detected for the aqueous dispersions of the as-prepared CQDs under excitation at 395 nm, is consistent with to the visible blue color of the same solutions under UV illumination (395 nm) as shown by the digital pictures in Fig. 2b.

The fluorescence excitation spectra of the as-prepared CQDs in water were recorded using an excitation wavelength (λex) of 500 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 10 . The obtained spectra are in good accordance with the corresponding UV–vis spectra (Fig. 6). For both the as-prepared CQDs, three peaks are observed at 280, 317/337 and 360 nm for the excited state; the band observable at 360 nm in the excitation spectra of both the as-prepared CQDs is probably hidden under the broad shoulder at 315 nm in the UV absorbance. The same most likely occurs for the excitation signal at 280 nm. It is interesting to note that the band at 260 nm in the UV–vis spectra is not belonged to any emitting species.

Fig. 11 a–b shows the PL decay profiles of the as-prepared CQDs, which were obtained by recording the transitions at different emission wavelengths, while the sample was excited at 405 nm by a laser diode. In particular, emissions at 460 nm, 490 nm and 550 nm were selected, due to the impossibility to collect the emission decay at a wavelength under or near that of laser (405 nm).

The deconvolution of the different curves shown in Fig. 11a has been obtained by a 3-components fitting, and the relative times and population are listed in Table 3 . For emission wavelength lower than 500 nm, the decay times for FX-CQDs are about 1 ns, 6 ns and 0.3 ns, with relative population of about 15%, 75% and 10% respectively. Only collecting the decay at emission wavelengths above 500 nm, the relative population are quite different, even though the decay times are very similar to those detected at lower emission wavelengths. In particular, we observe the growing of the relative population of the faster decay time (0.02 ns) from 10% for 460–490 nm emissions, to 53% for 550 nm emission. This behaviour could be attributed to some intrinsic phenomenon in the system rather than to the presence of a different emitting species. A similar behaviour was also observed for the FB-CQDs. In particular, Fig. 11b shows the decay time at the same emission wavelengths (460, 490, 550 nm), whereas Table 4 lists the deconvolution times and relative population for the corresponding 3-components fitting. Also in the case of FB-CQDs, the decay times corresponding to 460 and 490 nm emissions are very similar, whereas the excitation of the system at wavelengths above 500 nm results in the increase of the relative population (from 10% to 40%) associated with the faster decay time (0.02 ns).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties as a fuel of raw and thermally treated sugar beet pulp
ce:section.ce:para: The main fuel properties (i.e., the proximate composition, the ultimate composition and the calorific value) of the raw and thermally treated SBP are listed in Table 5 along with the mass yields of the latter ones. Data show that the volatile matter (VM) in the thermally treated biomass decreases, whereas the fixed carbon (FC) and the ash content increase. It is worth noting that the higher fuel ratio (i.e., fixed carbon to volatile matter contents) of thermally treated biomass compared to that of the raw biomass can lead to a more stable combustion process [53]. In addition, it was found that the mild thermal treatment undergone by SBP led to the reduction of its oxygen to carbon (O/C) and hydrogen to carbon (H/C) ratios, thus making it more suitable for fuel applications, particularly due to the resulting less smoke and water vapour formation, and the reduced energy loss during subsequent combustion and gasification processes [54]. The chemical composition changes induced by such thermal treatment also give rise to an increase in the calorific value of biomass, which makes it more compatible with coal co-firing (see Table 5). As a matter of the fact, unlike other currently available bottom-up techniques, a high-value by-product is obtained during the synthesis of biomass-based CDs assisted by γ-Al2O3, i.e. the residual thermally-treated biomass, which can be further valorised as a high quality solid biofuel exhibiting a lower oxygen-to-carbon ratio and, therefore, a higher calorific value compared to the parent biomass. In a properly designed and operated synthesis process, such residual biomass may be combusted to partially deliver the process heat demand, thus increasing its overall efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of formation of CQDs on γ-Al2O3 catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper understanding of the mechanism of formation of biomass-based CQDs assisted by γ-Al2O3 an additional test was performed, where a rock wool septum was inserted in the reactor to keep the biomass separated from the alumina particles whilst letting the evolved volatiles pass through the fluidized alumina bed. The finding that the CQDs formation also occurred in such arrangement, suggests that the synthesis of biomass-based CQDs on the γ-Al2O3 surface is a heterogeneous process, which occurs at the gas-solid interface rather than at the solid-solid interface.

Further insights on the mechanism of formation of CQDs on γ-Al2O3 catalyst can also be borrowed from the studies by Guisnet and Magnoux on the formation of carbonaceous deposits (“coke”) over acid and over bifunctional noble metal-acid catalysts [55]. These studies, in particular, demonstrated that, at low reaction temperatures (< 200 °C), the “coke” formation mainly involves condensation and rearrangement reactions. Accordingly, the deposits are not polyaromatic and their composition depends very much on the reactant(s) and on the reaction products. It should be emphasized that at low reaction temperatures, the retention of the “coke” molecules on the catalyst surface is mainly due to their strong adsorption and to their low volatility (gas-phase reactions) or to their low solubility (liquid-phase reactions). Conversely, at high reaction temperatures (> 350 °C), the carbonaceous deposits formed on acidic and bifunctional noble metal/acid catalysts are polyaromatict. Their composition, which is practically independent of the reactant, is mainly determined by the size and the shape of the pore, at least for microporous catalysts. The formation of these polyaromatic deposits involves hydrogen transfer (acid catalysts) and dehydrogenation (bifunctional catalysts) reactions in addition to the condensation and rearrangement ones. At intermediate temperature (200–350 °C), “coke formation” occurs through both the high temperature and the low temperature modes.

In light of the above-mentioned research findings, it is believed that in order to successfully exploit the intrinsic coking tendency of acid and bifunctional catalysts to promote the formation of self-functionalized carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) it is important not only to ensure the presence of a hydrocarbon gas stream rich in heteroatomic compounds containing oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) atoms, which is the case of the volatiles evolved during the mild thermal treatment of sugar beet pulp residues [25], but also to operate under mild reaction temperatures, which limit the degree of aromaticity of carbon deposites fromed during the hydrocarbon transformations. It is worth noting that, even though the reaction temperature plays a predominant role, the composition of "coke" also depends on all the other characteristics of the catalytic system, including type of catalyst, nature of the feed, and operating conditions. The composition of non-desorbed products depends also very much on the amount retained on the catalyst; the higher this amount, hence the longer the “time-on-stream” in flow reactors or the “residence time” in batch reactors, the greater the complexity and the polyaromaticity of the non-desorbed products. In addition, the composition of these products depends significantly on the nature of active sites (acid, base, acid–base, metal, etc.), which determines the reaction steps, which can be catalyzed. Further research efforts are, therefore, required to better understand and evaluate the impact of operative parameters on the process performance.

Nevertheless, the innovative finding that a catalyst can be used to produce CQDs from the volatile matter that is driven off the biomass by heating, opens up new possibilities of valorization for the off-gas substream coming from other biomass conversion processes, like for example torrefaction, slow pyrolysis and carbonization.

An innovative route for the sustainable and environmental-friendly synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is presented in this work, which relies on the use of the fluidized bed technology and commercial γ-alumina as a catalyst, for the first time.

Results of this study represents an important leap in CQDs research area, since they shows that fluorescent carbon nanoparticles can be produced from readily available and cheap feedstocks through an easily applicable synthetic route with no need for sophisticated and/or expensive instrumentation as occurs, for example, in the case of electrochemical methodologies or laser ablation.

In addition, the innovative finding that a catalyst can be used to produce CQDs from the volatile matter driven off from the biomass under a mild thermal treatment, opens up new possibilities of valorisation for the off-gas substream (pyrolytic volatiles, torgas, tar, etc) coming from other biomass conversion process, like for example torrefaction, slow pyrolysis and carbonization.

However, further investigations are needed to better characterize the properties of the obtained biomass-based CQDs (e.g., quantum yield, stability, surface charge and so on), which are fundamental to establish their practical applicability. Additional research efforts are also required to shed light on the relationship between the operating conditions of the presented catalytic thermal synthesis (biomass-to-catalyst mass ratio, temperature, reaction time, alumina particle size and porosity, gas-solid contact time, etc.) and the Carbon-Dots’ surface chemistry and optical properties.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insight into the excitation‐dependent fluorescence of carbon dots
Chem. Phys. Chem.

Nanomaterials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocluster superstructures or nanoparticles? The self-consuming scaffold decides
Nanoscale

The self-consuming scaffold decides, Nanoscale 10 (2018) pp. 7472-7483. https://doi.org/10.1039/C7NR09520A.

Commun., 52 (2016) pp. 11912-11914. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6CC06673A

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in carbon nanodots
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.
